old_claim.note: Mercantilism Economic Theory provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Mercantilist colonial policy commercially structured triangular trade by treating colonies as raw-material suppliers and captive markets for European manufactured goods.

source_shape.source_locator: Mercantilism article: Britannica says colonies supplied raw materials to mother countries and bought finished goods from them, while the triangular-trade article describes European manufactured goods, enslaved Africans, and colonial raw materials circulating under European colonial powers.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Mercantilism structured colonial raw-material and manufactured-goods exchange, which matches the commercial organization of triangular trade.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports mercantilism as a commercial and policy framework for the trade system, not as a material or ship-design enabler.

ontology_before: The old edge treated mercantilist theory as a generic enabling capability for triangular trade.
ontology_after: The corrected edge models mercantilism as the colonial commercial framework that scaled and structured the triangular-trade exchange pattern.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: mercantilism_economic_theory is now a commercial_or_scaling_dependency rather than a direct enabling prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The triangular_trade node is later narrowed to individual voyages independent of colonial commercial policy.
A stronger source shows mercantilism was technically necessary for each leg of triangular trade.
The graph adds a more specific colonial-trade-regulation node that should replace mercantilism on this edge.
short_reason: Mercantilism structured colonial trade commercially rather than supplying a direct technical capability.
